facts_disclosed: Great Ocean Road camping trip with friends, Easter weekend April 18-21, 2025, camping at Johanna Beach, needs to borrow a tent

turns.content: ok i need your help planning something fun for once instead of having an existential crisis lol. were doing a road trip for easter and i am SO keen

I love that energy! A road trip sounds like exactly the right kind of reset.

Tell me the basics — where are you heading, how many people, and how many days? And are we talking camping, Airbnb, or sleeping-in-the-car vibes?

great ocean road!! april 18-21 so the easter long weekend. camping at johanna beach. theres like 6 of us going, mix of uni friends and a couple of people from work. its going to be heaps good i genuinely cannot wait

Johanna Beach is a stunning choice — it's one of the less crowded spots along the Great Ocean Road, which is great because Easter long weekend can get absolutely packed at the more popular campgrounds. The beach is wild and beautiful, and the stars at night there are incredible with minimal light pollution.

A few practical things to sort out:

**Campsite booking:**
- Johanna Beach campground is managed by Parks Victoria. Easter is peak season — have you booked already? If not, do it ASAP because those sites fill up fast over long weekends.

**Getting there:**
- Melbourne to Johanna Beach is about 3-3.5 hours via the inland route (M1 then Princes Highway), or longer if you drive the Great Ocean Road the whole way (scenic but slower, especially with Easter traffic)
- Are you driving the GOR route one way and inland the other? That's the classic move.

**Gear:**
- Camping gear sorted? Tents, sleeping bags, cooking setup?
- Johanna Beach is fairly basic — there are drop toilets and fire pits but no powered sites, no showers. It's proper camping.

What's your gear situation looking like?

ok so the campsite is booked, my friend jess organised that ages ago thank god. were driving down the great ocean road on the way there and doing the inland route back which is exactly what you said lol.

gear wise... so this is slightly embarrassing but i need to borrow a tent because mine has a massive hole in the floor from last time. like it rained and i just woke up in a puddle. very fun. sleeping bag is fine though

Waking up in a puddle is a formative camping experience that everyone should have exactly once. You've had yours — time to upgrade (or at least borrow).

For the tent situation:
- **Ask your group first** — with 6 people, someone might have a spare tent, or you could share with someone who has a 3-person tent
- **If no one has a spare:** try Facebook Marketplace, Gumtree, or even your uni's buy/swap/sell group. People sell barely-used tents cheap after festival season
- **Rent option:** some outdoor gear shops (like Bogong in the city or Mountain Equipment in Richmond) do tent rentals for about $30-50 for a long weekend
- **Budget buy:** Kmart and Decathlon sell basic 2-person tents for $50-80 that are fine for a 3-night trip in April

April on the coast is autumn, so expect:
- Daytime: 15-18°C, pleasant
- Nighttime: 8-12°C, chilly
- Rain is possible — make sure whatever tent you borrow actually keeps water out this time

Do you need help with a camping food/supplies checklist too, or is that sorted?

im going to ask around the group first cos buying a tent feels excessive when i know like 10 people who camp. someone will have one. and yes PLEASE to a food checklist because last time we went camping the food situation was tragic. like we brought chips and vibes and that was it

Chips and vibes is a bold catering strategy. Let's do better this time.

**Camping food checklist for 3 nights (your share — coordinate with the group so you're not all bringing bread):**

**Breakfast:**
- Oats + dried fruit + nuts (just add hot water from the camp kettle)
- Bread + peanut butter + banana for easy mornings
- Instant coffee or bring a stovetop percolator if anyone has one

**Lunch:**
- Wraps + hummus + veggies (pre-chop carrots, capsicum, cucumber before you go)
- Trail mix for snacking
- Fruit that travels well — apples, oranges, mandarins

**Dinner:**
- **Night 1:** Pre-made pasta sauce (cook at home, freeze, it'll thaw in the esky and be ready to heat) + dried pasta
- **Night 2:** Campfire stir-fry — pre-cut veggies in a ziplock, noodles, soy sauce, done in one pan
- **Night 3:** Wraps/tacos — canned beans, pre-made salsa, avocado, whatever's left from lunch supplies

**Essentials:**
- Good esky with actual ice (frozen water bottles double as ice + drinking water)
- Soy sauce, salt, olive oil in small containers
- One sharp knife, one cutting board, one good pan
- Plates/bowls/cutlery — reusable, not disposable
- Bin bags — leave no trace
- A camp stove or confirm someone's bringing one

**Snacks/campfire:**
- Marshmallows for toasting (check ingredients if that matters for your group)
- Dark chocolate
- Popcorn kernels + a pot with a lid = campfire popcorn

The key is doing all the chopping and sauce-making at home before you go, so at camp you're just heating things up. Does this work for you?

campfire popcorn is an elite suggestion and i am absolutely doing that. also the frozen pasta sauce thing is genius... pre-make it, chuck it in the esky, it keeps everything cold AND becomes dinner. actual big brain move

The frozen sauce hack is one of those things that sounds obvious once you hear it but nobody thinks of it. It's doing double duty — insulation AND food. Efficiency queen behaviour.

One more tip for the esky: layer it properly. Ice/frozen stuff on the bottom, things you'll need first on top. And keep it in the shade with a wet towel on top — evaporative cooling extends the ice life significantly. If you're organized about opening it (get everything you need in one go instead of opening it 50 times), your ice will last all three days.

Also — for the drive down the Great Ocean Road, are you planning any stops? The classic ones are:
- **Torquay/Bells Beach** — quick look, stretch legs
- **Lorne** — good for a coffee stop
- **Apollo Bay** — last proper town before Johanna, good to grab any forgotten supplies
- **Twelve Apostles** — you've probably done it before but it's on the way

Or are you going straight through to get to camp and set up?

definitely stopping at apollo bay for supplies and coffee. and probably the twelve apostles cos a couple of the people coming havent seen them. i reckon we'll leave melbourne early on the friday so we have time for stops without rushing

Leaving early Friday is smart — you'll beat most of the Easter traffic if you're out of Melbourne by 8-9am. The GOR gets heavy from midday onwards on the Friday before Easter.

Apollo Bay is the perfect last stop — there's a decent IGA there for anything you forgot, and a few good coffee spots along the main street. Stock up on firewood there too if you're planning campfires, because Johanna Beach can be hit or miss for finding dry wood on-site.

For the Twelve Apostles, pro tip: if you're passing through in the afternoon, the light is actually better than morning for photos — the sun hits the limestone from the west. But honestly at any time of day it's impressive, especially for first-timers.

How are you splitting up the driving? Three and a half hours plus stops is a decent stretch.

my friend tom has a car thats big enough for all of us so were doing one car which keeps it simple. him and jess will probably split the driving cos theyre the most confident on the winding bits. i get carsick on the great ocean road which is very embarrassing but very real lol

No shame in the carsickness — the Great Ocean Road is notoriously twisty, especially the section between Lorne and Apollo Bay. A lot of people struggle with it.

A few things that genuinely help:
- **Sit in the front seat** if possible — you can see the road ahead, which reduces the mismatch between what your eyes see and what your inner ear feels
- **Look at the horizon**, not your phone. Reading or scrolling is the fastest way to trigger it on winding roads
- **Crack the window** for fresh air, even if it's cold
- **Ginger** — ginger chews, ginger beer, or ginger tablets before you get in. It's one of the few natural remedies with actual evidence behind it
- **Travel sickness tablets** (like Travacalm) work well but can make you drowsy — which honestly might be a feature, not a bug, for a passenger on a long drive

And the frequent stops at Torquay, Lorne, and Apollo Bay will help break it up so you're not doing a long stretch without a break.

the ginger thing is good actually i might grab some ginger chews. and honestly falling asleep in the car might be the move because then i wake up and were there. ok genuinely thank you for this whole checklist and everything because left to my own devices i would have showed up with chips, a broken tent, and optimism

"Chips, a broken tent, and optimism" would make an excellent camping memoir title.

Quick summary of your to-do list before April 18:

1. **Tent** — ask friends this week, don't leave it last minute
2. **Food coordination** — set up a shared doc or group chat list so people claim dishes and you don't end up with six loaves of bread
3. **Esky + ice** — buy bags of ice the morning you leave
4. **Pre-make and freeze** — pasta sauce, maybe a curry, anything that freezes well
5. **Campfire popcorn supplies** — kernels, oil, a pot
6. **Ginger chews** — for the Great Ocean Road curves
7. **Check you have basics** — torch/headlamp, warm layers for night, rain jacket just in case

You've got a month to sort it. That's heaps of time. This trip is going to be great — Johanna Beach at Easter with good people and actual food is a genuinely solid plan.

im going to screenshot this entire conversation and put it in the group chat. jess will be so impressed that i actually planned something for once instead of just being the friend who shows up and eats everyone elses food lol. ok im keen as, one month to go!! thanks heaps

Ha! Character development: going from chips-and-vibes camper to the one with the frozen pasta sauce and campfire popcorn plan. Jess won't know what hit her.

Have an amazing time at Johanna Beach — and seriously, sort the tent this week while you're motivated. Future you, sleeping dry and warm on night one, will be very grateful.

Enjoy the Great Ocean Road!
